    "RX.1": 1.0,
    "RX.2": 1.0,
    "RX.3": 1.0,
    "RX.4": 1.0,
    "RX.5": 1.0
  },
  "active_set": ["R1.2", "R1.3", "R1.7", "RX.1", "RX.2", "RX.3", "RX.4", "RX.5"],
  "category_focus": "R1",
  "clarification_needed": true,
  "clarification_question": "To focus the check, should I prioritize (A) verifying logical consistency and claim–evidence alignment (including citation/attribution accuracy), (B) sentence-level writing polish, or (C) novelty/positioning versus nearest prior work? You can choose one or more.",
  "rationale": {
    "R1.2": "“Check this” most naturally implies verifying the internal logic connecting the premises, intermediate claims, and conclusions across the excerpt.",
    "R1.3": "The passage reports quantitative effects (QoS, cost, commitment) that should be checked for alignment with the described experiments and ablations.",
    "R1.7": "Named prior work (Achiam et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2025) is cited as theoretical grounding, so attribution and claim–citation accuracy warrant attention.",
    "RX.1": "Given the brevity of the request and excerpt, uncertainty about the intended scope should be made explicit.",
    "RX.2": "Claims about horizon-invariance and ‘fundamental architectural difference’ should be checked to avoid overstating generality beyond the provided evidence.",
    "RX.3": "The review should adhere strictly to checking the provided text rather than expanding into redesigning experiments or the broader paper.",
    "RX.4": "Guarding against potentially misleading interpretations is important because overconfident claims about safety-related constraints can have downstream risks.",
    "RX.5": "Any assessments should be traceable to specific sentences and cited sources to maintain auditability."
  }
}

parsed.clarification_question: To focus the check, should I prioritize (A) verifying logical consistency and claim–evidence alignment (including citation/attribution accuracy), (B) sentence-level writing polish, or (C) novelty/positioning versus nearest prior work? You can choose one or more.

rationale.R1.2: “Check this” most naturally implies verifying the internal logic connecting the premises, intermediate claims, and conclusions across the excerpt.
rationale.R1.3: The passage reports quantitative effects (QoS, cost, commitment) that should be checked for alignment with the described experiments and ablations.
rationale.R1.7: Named prior work (Achiam et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2025) is cited as theoretical grounding, so attribution and claim–citation accuracy warrant attention.
rationale.RX.1: Given the brevity of the request and excerpt, uncertainty about the intended scope should be made explicit.
rationale.RX.2: Claims about horizon-invariance and ‘fundamental architectural difference’ should be checked to avoid overstating generality beyond the provided evidence.
rationale.RX.3: The review should adhere strictly to checking the provided text rather than expanding into redesigning experiments or the broader paper.
rationale.RX.4: Guarding against potentially misleading interpretations is important because overconfident claims about safety-related constraints can have downstream risks.
rationale.RX.5: Any assessments should be traceable to specific sentences and cited sources to maintain auditability.
